content.problemStatement:
You are given two positive integers NNN and XXX.

Find any permutation PPP of the integers 111 to NNN such that the number of indices iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N) satisfying

Pi‚ą£i=XP_i \mid i = XPi‚Äč‚ą£i=Xis as large as possible.

Here, ‚ą£\mid‚ą£ denotes the bitwise OR operation.

A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.

If there are multiple permutations that maximize the required count of indices, you may find any of them.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX ‚Äď the length of the permutation, and the target bitwise OR.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers, denoting the permutation you found.

If there are multiple valid answers, all of them will be accepted.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We have N=5N = 5N=5 and X=1X = 1X=1. With P=[1,3,2,5,4]P = [1, 3, 2, 5, 4]P=[1,3,2,5,4], we have P1‚ą£1=1‚ą£1=1P_1 \mid 1 = 1 \mid 1 = 1P1‚Äč‚ą£1=1‚ą£1=1, so there's one valid index.
It can be shown that it's not possible to obtain more than one valid index.
Test case 222: No matter what permutation is taken, there will be no indices satisfying Pi‚ą£i=4P_i\mid i = 4Pi‚Äč‚ą£i=4, so any permutation of [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] is a valid answer here.
Test case 333: With P=[3,4,1,2]P = [3, 4, 1, 2]P=[3,4,1,2], there are two indices (i=2i = 2i=2 and i=4i = 4i=4) for which Pi‚ą£i=XP_i \mid i = XPi‚Äč‚ą£i=X. This is optimal.
